guides.name: Choosing the right primitive
guides.summary: Choose Salt components, layouts, and patterns by user intent first, then prefer the most constrained Salt option before creating custom UI.

steps.title: Start with user intent
statements: Trigger work in the current view: use Button.
Navigate to another route, page, or document: use Link.
Show or hide related content: use a disclosure primitive such as Accordion or Collapsible.
Structure a page or region: start with layouts.
Build a repeated multi-part flow: check patterns before inventing a new composition.

steps.title: Common decisions
statements: Use Button for actions such as save, apply, retry, or open dialog.
Use Link for real destinations such as routes, pages, help articles, or document downloads.
Start with a component when you need one UI element with clear semantics.
Start with a pattern when the job spans multiple components or repeated page structure.
Prefer the Salt primitive first.
Add a wrapper only when it adds stable semantics, shared behavior, or a real domain API.

steps.title: Read next
statements: Button usage
Link usage
Layouts overview
Patterns overview
Composition pitfalls
Custom wrappers

guides.name: Composition pitfalls
aliases: Avoid pass-through wrappers over Salt primitives
guides.summary: Review the most common Salt composition mistakes, including nested interactive primitives, pass-through wrappers, and rebuilding standard primitives.

steps.title: Compose to simplify
statements: make intent clearer
reduce repeated code
preserve the semantics of the underlying Salt primitives
keep structure shallow unless deeper nesting is necessary for the interaction

steps.title: Pitfalls to avoid
statements: Do not nest interactive components such as Button and Link inside each other. Choose one interactive owner for the action or navigation.
Treat wrappers that only forward props to a Salt primitive as suspicious. If a wrapper does not add behavior, semantics, or a stable domain API, prefer using the Salt primitive directly.
Avoid rebuilding Salt buttons, links, navigation items, or other common components from raw HTML, custom roles, and ad hoc styling when Salt already provides the correct primitive.

Avoid stacking multiple layout containers when one Salt layout component can express the structure more clearly. Each wrapper should have a clear job such as controlling spacing, flow, split regions, or parent-child arrangement.
Avoid raw spacing, sizing, and color values that override Salt foundations and token usage without a strong reason. Prefer Salt layouts, foundations, and tokens before introducing custom styling.

steps.title: Before and after
statements: If a wrapper remains, it should add more than prop forwarding. Give it a clear reason such as shared behavior, domain semantics, or a stable public API.

snippets.title: Before and after example 1
Before and after example 2
Before and after example 3
Before and after example 4
Before and after example 5
Before and after example 6

steps.title: When wrappers are justified
statements: establishes a stable domain-specific API
adds shared behavior such as analytics, permissions, or data wiring
adds semantics that the base primitive does not carry on its own
coordinates multiple Salt primitives into a reusable higher-level unit
If the wrapper is justified, keep the responsibilities explicit and avoid hiding the underlying interaction model.
This wrapper adds a domain-specific API and shared behavior rather than only renaming Button.

snippets.title: When wrappers are justified example 1

steps.title: Quick review checklist
statements: Is the interaction owned by the right Salt primitive?
Are there nested interactive Salt primitives that should be flattened?
Is there a pass-through wrapper over a Salt primitive that should be removed?
Could a Salt layout or pattern replace some of the custom structure?
Does the styling still follow Salt foundations and token guidance?

steps.title: Next reads
statements: Choosing the right primitive
Custom wrappers
Developing with Salt
Layouts overview
Patterns overview

guides.name: Custom wrappers
aliases: Avoid pass-through wrappers over Salt primitives
guides.summary: Learn when a wrapper over a Salt primitive adds value and when it is only hiding the underlying component behind prop forwarding.

steps.title: When wrappers help
statements: add a stable domain-specific API
coordinate permissions, analytics, or data wiring
add semantics that the primitive does not carry on its own
combine several Salt primitives into a reusable higher-level unit

steps.title: When wrappers hurt
statements: only forward props to a single Salt primitive
hide the real interaction owner
recreate an existing Salt primitive with a different name
make it harder to find the canonical Salt usage guidance

steps.title: Avoid pass-through wrappers over Salt primitives
statements: Treat pass-through wrappers as suspicious. If the wrapper only spreads props into Button, Link, or another Salt primitive, prefer using the primitive directly.

snippets.title: Avoid pass-through wrappers over Salt primitives example 1
Avoid pass-through wrappers over Salt primitives example 2

steps.title: Prefer explicit added behavior
statements: If a wrapper remains, make the added value obvious in its API and implementation.
This wrapper adds domain behavior and a clearer business API instead of just renaming Button.

snippets.title: Prefer explicit added behavior example 1

steps.title: Wrapper review checklist
statements: Does it add behavior, semantics, or a stable domain API?
Would direct use of the Salt primitive be clearer?
Is the interaction owner still obvious from the JSX?
Does the wrapper preserve Salt guidance instead of obscuring it?

statements: Composition pitfalls
Choosing the right primitive
Button usage

guides.name: Developing with Salt
aliases: Before you build screens and flows
guides.summary: Learn how to install Salt, bootstrap a React app with the recommended JPM Brand theme, and start importing components intentionally.

steps.title: Install the Salt packages
statements: @salt-ds/core contains production-ready UI components.
@salt-ds/theme contains CSS files that apply Salt's default theme.
@salt-ds/icons contains SVG-based icons.
If you prefer not to use npm, you can install the packages using yarn or other package managers.

steps.title: Add required web fonts
statements: Salt uses the Open Sans and PT Mono web fonts. If you follow the recommended JPM Brand setup below, also make the Amplitude font available for headingFont="Amplitude" and actionFont="Amplitude". See Themes for the matching @font-face example and the Legacy theme setup.
Refer to the Google Fonts page for more information.
With Fontsource, you can include the web font files into your build and self-host them.
Learn more about Fontsource.

If you prefer not to use Google Fonts or Fontsource, you can use any other method to incorporate the web fonts. Make sure the CSS @font-face declarations associate the correct font-family, font-weightandfont-style with the corresponding web fonts.

snippets.title: Add required web fonts example 1
snippets.code:
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin />
<link
  href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Open+Sans:ital,wght@0,300;0,400;0,500;0,600;0,700;0,800;1,300;1,400;1,500;1,600;1,700;1,800&family=PT+Mono&display=swap"
  rel="stylesheet"
/>

snippets.title: Add required web fonts example 3

steps.title: Integrate Salt into your app
statements: For new projects, import the Salt theme CSS, add the JPM Brand theme CSS, and wrap your application in SaltProviderNext with the default brand props.

snippets.title: Integrate Salt into your app example 1

steps.title: Import components
statements: Now that you’re ready to use our UI components, you can import them from the @salt-ds/core package and use them in your JSX code.
Head over to our available components to view their respective APIs.

snippets.title: Import components example 1

steps.title: (Optional) Play with lab components
statements: Our @salt-ds/lab package is where we introduce new components that are in the initial stages of development. Once they’re stable, we move them over to the core package.

Because it’s an unstable environment that may cause major breaking changes, we don’t recommend using lab components in production applications. However, early adopters are welcome to use and experiment with them.
We're keen to hear your feedback, bug reports and feature requests, so please don't hesitate to raise new issues or comment on existing ones.

guides.name: Themes
guides.summary: A theme is a collection of design tokens that define the overall look and feel of an interface. Themes allow you to quickly switch the visual style of components to match different brands or user preferences, ensuring consistency across your application.

steps.title: Choose a Salt theme
statements: Salt offers the default JPM Brand theme for new work and a Legacy theme for UITK migration paths.
Salt recommends the JPM Brand theme for accessibility and long-term brand alignment.

steps.title: Apply the JPM Brand theme
statements: The JPM Brand theme is Salt’s long-term visual style, designed to match the JPMorgan look across CIB digital applications. Developed with Corporate Brand and CIB Marketing, it uses approved styles for consistent visuals on desktop and web. Salt recommends switching to the JPM Brand theme for better accessibility and brand alignment.
Use SaltProviderNext with accent="teal", corner="rounded", headingFont="Amplitude", actionFont="Amplitude" for the default new-project brand mapping.
Import "@salt-ds/theme/index.css" and "@salt-ds/theme/css/theme-next.css" before rendering the provider.
Ensure the host app loads Amplitude when using headingFont="Amplitude" and actionFont="Amplitude".

snippets.title: JPM Brand theme
Amplitude font-face declarations

steps.title: Apply the Legacy theme
statements: For a gradual move from the UITK system, Salt’s Legacy (UITK) theme can be used with UITK components to support different migration timelines and needs. J.P. Morgan employees can find more details on migration options and system comparisons.
Use SaltProvider with "@salt-ds/theme/index.css" when compatibility or migration work must stay on the Legacy theme.
